texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper is Part 7 of the post-combustion carbon capture technology Review Series. It reviews recent progress and developments in hollow fibre membrane contactor research for CO 2 capture. Hollow fiber membrane contactors have been studied for CO 2 capture from a gas stream since 1985. In recent years, this technology has been considered a promising alternative to conventional absorption technologies, since it offers higher absorption efficiency and avoids common operating problems found in traditional packed columns. In this review, research development focused on CO 2 capture using hollow fiber membrane contactors - including membrane  module  design,  mass  transfer  principles,  membrane  wetting,  simulation  and  modeling,  and solvent regeneration - is presented. Additionally, current significant pilot-scale applications of this technology are discussed and recommendations for future work are presented.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2   is  one  of  the  major  GHGs,  is  a  significant contributor to climate change. As a result, the capture of CO 2 has been widely studied over the past few decades. In order to mitigate CO 2 emissions from various industrial gas streams, efficient and cost-effective technologies are needed for CO 2 capture. Traditional technologies for gas separation include absorption into a liquid solvent, adsorption onto a solid sorbent, cryogenic separation, membrane separation and chemical conversion. Among them, chemical absorption into a liquid solvent using packed columns is considered to be the most suitable technology for CO 2 capture from various gas streams, especially flue gas [1] . For example, in a packed column the absorption liquid is dispersed into a large contact area that interacts with the gas, resulting in a high rate of mass transfer. However, the capture of CO 2 in packed columns is energy intensive and restricted to a limited range of gas and liquid flow velocities because of operating problems, including flooding, channeling, foaming and entrainment [2] .
texts.text: CO2   is  one  of  the  major  GHGs,  is  a  significant contributor to climate change. As a result, the capture of CO 2 has been widely studied over the past few decades. In order to mitigate CO 2 emissions from various industrial gas streams, efficient and cost-effective technologies are needed for CO 2 capture. Traditional technologies for gas separation include absorption into a liquid solvent, adsorption onto a solid sorbent, cryogenic separation, membrane separation and chemical conversion. Among them, chemical absorption into a liquid solvent using packed columns is considered to be the most suitable technology for CO 2 capture from various gas streams, especially flue gas [1] . For example, in a packed column the absorption liquid is dispersed into a large contact area that interacts with the gas, resulting in a high rate of mass transfer. However, the capture of CO 2 in packed columns is energy intensive and restricted to a limited range of gas and liquid flow velocities because of operating problems, including flooding, channeling, foaming and entrainment [2] .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactors equipped with hydrophobic hollow fiber membranes have been considered a promising  alternative  to  traditional  absorption  columns for CO 2 capture in recent years [3-6] .  Traditional gas separation membrane technology is limited by its low selectivity.  Membrane contactors combine chemical absorption with membrane separation technology to create small reactors with high selectivity [7] . The hollow fiber membranes in a gas-liquid membrane contactor provide a barrier between the gas and liquid phases. The absorption liquid offers the selectivity, rather than the membrane. As a result, the driving force for gas separation is a concentration gradient instead of a pressure gradient. As shown in Figure 1 , absorption occurs once the gas diffuses through the porous membrane and contacts the solvent at the surface of the membrane on the liquid side of the contactor. This allows for separate control of the liquid and gas velocities, avoiding operating problems that are frequently encountered in traditional absorption columns. Additionally, hollow fiber membranes offer a much larger surface area per unit volume than traditional packed columns, which provides an opportunity to achieve very high mass transfer rates.

texts.orig: hollow fiber membrane contractor. Research results have shown the CO 2 absorption performance in a membrane contactor can be up to nine-times higher than that in a packed column [3,10] . Yan et al. conducted a comparative ana  lysis of CO 2 absorption performance by using a hollow fiber membrane contactor and a packed column [11] . Their results suggested that if the membrane pores were not completely wetted, or less than 50% of the pores were  not  plugged,  a  hollow  fiber membrane contactor could perform better than a packed column. As an added benefit, the size of the membrane contactor may be up to 60% smaller than a packed column [12] .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hollow fiber membrane contactors also have several disadvantages. First, the very presence of the membrane in  the  contactor  adds  additional  resistance  to  mass transfer. Typically, this would result in a reduction in the overall mass transfer rate. However, having a very large interfacial mass transfer area can potentially result in a sufficiently high mass transfer rate that overcomes the negative impact of the membrane resistance and results in a net increase in mass transfer.

texts.orig: Another major challenge with membrane contactors is the issue of membrane wetting . A number of studies have found the absorption/regeneration performance in hollow fiber membrane contactor may reduce rapidly and dramatically when the membrane becomes partially wetted by the absorption liquid [10,14-17] .  Membrane wetting is a significant research challenge that needs to  be  solved  in  order  to  commercialize hollow fiber membrane contactor technology.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This  comprehensive  review  summarizes  previous research efforts and provides a critical view of CO 2 capture using hollow fiber membrane contactors. Many significant research advances are discussed on a number of topics, including membrane contactor design, mass transfer principles, membrane wetting, simulation and modeling, and membrane contactors for solvent regeneration . In addition, current applications are discussed and future research is proposed.

texts.orig: and membrane module arrangement. In real applications of membrane contactors for CO 2 absorption, the need for a large membrane contact area has implications for the membrane length and diameter. The design of membrane cascade systems may be a good option for achieving the desired contact area [19,20] . To date, many research efforts have focused on developing an effective membrane module [13,21,22] . The major aspects of membrane module design include the spacing of the fibers, membrane fiber packing density and the relative two-phase fluid flow directions on the shell and lumen sides, such as parallel and cross flows. The relative twophase fluid flow directions in parallel-flow systems can be con- or counter-current. Results have shown that counter-current flow offers mass transfer coefficients that are on average 20% higher than con-current flow [16,23] . Table 1 lists some current research results on the overall mass transfer rate/coefficient for CO 2 absorption in gas-liquid membrane contactors. Based on the relative flow direction of the fluids, membrane modules can be classified as parallel or cross flow.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig:  Parallel-flow modules
texts.text:  Parallel-flow modules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Parallel-flow modules are widely adopted in hollow fiber membrane contactors for CO 2 capture because of their simplicity in manufacturing and suitability for predicting mass transfer rates [11,12,15,24-31] . In such a module, the gas phase on the opposite side of the membrane fibers flows parallel to the liquid phase. Figure 2 shows a schematic diagram for a parallel-flow module. This flow module is preferred when mass transfer is restricted by the membrane itself [23] . However, parallel-flow  modules offer  moderate mass transfer performance when compared with cross-flow modules because the mass transfer process for CO 2 capture in a hollow fiber membrane contactor is controlled by the liquid phase when the membrane is not wetted.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The cross-flow module was first investigated for gas stripping processes. Comparative ana  lysis of a number of cross-flow modules with a cylindrical parallel-flow module indicated that the cross-flow modules were substantially more efficient than parallel-flow modules, since it can reduce fluid channeling and increase local turbulence levels [32-34] .  As a result, cross-flow modules with hollow fiber membranes have been extensively studied for CO 2 capture [21,22] .  TNO (Delft, The Netherlands) developed a rectangular cross-flow design of a membrane module for CO 2 capture from flue gas [35-37] .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 3 shows a cross-flow module where the gas stream flows on the shell side, perpendicular to the fibers, and the absorption liquid flows through the membrane fibers. In this schematic, the overall flow pattern of the module is counter-current, but the relative flow of the two phases is cross flow. The advantages of this design include high mass transfer rates, low-pressure drop, well defined flow conditions on the shell and lumen sides and easy scale-up. Dindore and Versteeg from the University of Twente (Enschede, The Netherlands) designed a similar cross-flow module for CO 2 capture as shown in Figure 4 [34] .  Analytical modules based on heat transfer analogies were developed to describe the mass transfer process in the cross-flow module. The experimental results were comparable to the results predicted by the analytical model [38,39] .

texts.orig: Recently, a baffled hollow fiber membrane contactor with cross-flow characteristics was designed and tested for CO 2 capture from a mixture of CO 2 and air [13] . As shown in Figure 5 , several baffles were installed on the shell side of the membrane module. In this system, the gas stream flowed on the shell side and liquid entered the contactor on the lumen side. The integration of baffles into the module was intended to improve the gas flow by converting parallel flow into cross flow. A small improvement of 5% in the overall mass transfer coefficient was observed when compared with an unbaffled module operated under the same experimental conditions [13] .  Such a minor improvement was attributed to the small impact of the gas phase on the overall mass transfer. The Liqui-Cel ®   membrane contactor is a commercially available baffled hollow fiber membrane contactor, which provides shell-side cross flow to yield a high liquid-phase mass transfer coefficient. This membrane contactor has been utilized for pilot-scale/ laboratory-scale CO 2 absorption from real/simulated flue gases [5,40,41] .
texts.text: Recently, a baffled hollow fiber membrane contactor with cross-flow characteristics was designed and tested for CO 2 capture from a mixture of CO 2 and air [13] . As shown in Figure 5 , several baffles were installed on the shell side of the membrane module. In this system, the gas stream flowed on the shell side and liquid entered the contactor on the lumen side. The integration of baffles into the module was intended to improve the gas flow by converting parallel flow into cross flow. A small improvement of 5% in the overall mass transfer coefficient was observed when compared with an unbaffled module operated under the same experimental conditions [13] .  Such a minor improvement was attributed to the small impact of the gas phase on the overall mass transfer. The Liqui-Cel ®   membrane contactor is a commercially available baffled hollow fiber membrane contactor, which provides shell-side cross flow to yield a high liquid-phase mass transfer coefficient. This membrane contactor has been utilized for pilot-scale/ laboratory-scale CO 2 absorption from real/simulated flue gases [5,40,41] .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mass transfer is a process in which one component transfers from one phase to another phase, or through the same phase, due to a concentration gradient [42] . Gas absorption into a liquid takes place when mass transfer occurs between a gas phase and a liquid phase. Film theory, introduced by Whitman, is one of the most widely used models for describing the gas absorption process [43] . It assumes the gas and liquid are in equilibrium at the interface, and two thin films at the interface separate the gas and liquid phases. Mass transfer occurs through the thin films by molecular diffusion. The driving force for mass transfer is the concentration difference between the solute and solvent.

Gas absorption into a liquid takes place when mass transfer occurs between a gas phase and a liquid phase. Film theory, introduced by Whitman, is one of the most widely used models for describing the gas absorption process [43] . It assumes the gas and liquid are in equilibrium at the interface, and two thin films at the interface separate the gas and liquid phases. Mass transfer occurs through the thin films by molecular diffusion. The driving force for mass transfer is the concentration difference between the solute and solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a hollow fiber membrane contactor, the absorption of CO 2 into liquid happens via three continuous steps: CO 2 diffusion from the gas phase to the mouth of the membrane pores; continuous diffusion through membrane pores; and dissolution and diffusion into the liquid phase with/without chemical reaction [6] . As shown in Figure 6 , CO2 must diffuse through the membrane pores first, prior to contacting the absorption liquid. For traditional absorption technologies, for example packed columns, there are two mass transfer resistances: liquid phase and gas phase mass transfer resistance. For membrane absorption contactors, there is membrane phase mass transfer resistance besides the liquid phase and gas phase mass transfer resistance.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Membrane wetting
texts.text: Membrane wetting

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Three modes of operation for CO 2 absorption in hollow membrane fiber contactors are reported in the literature: nonwetted, completely wetted and partially wetted. In the nonwetted mode, the membrane pores remain completely gas filled over a prolonged period of operation time. In the completely or partially wetted modes, the membrane pores totally or partially fill with the absorption liquid over time. In both wetted operation modes, the mass transfer resistance caused by the liquid in the membrane pores is much higher than that caused by the gas in the membrane pores. As a result, the wetted membrane pores significantly decrease the overall mass transfer coefficient and affect the stability and long-term operation of hollow fiber membrane contactors [55] . Mavroudi et al. found the mass transfer resistance caused by the liquid-filled membrane pores could account for more than 98% of the total membrane resistance when only 13% of the membrane pores were wetted [41] . Wang et al. developed a theoretical model to simulate CO 2 absorption into water using a PP hollow fiber membrane contactor [55] . Simulation results showed that if 5% of the membrane pores were wetted, the overall mass transfer coefficient may reduce by 20%. Thus, the nonwetted mode of membrane operation is preferred for CO 2 absorption since it has a much lower membrane mass transfer resistance when compared with the wetted modes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Currently, CO 2 absorption in hollow fiber membrane contactors usually pairs hydrophobic membranes such as polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), PP, polyethylene (PE) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) with organic solvents that have low surface tensions. Large amounts of research on CO 2 absorption in hollow fiber membrane contactors has demonstrated that gradual wetting of hydrophobic membranes cannot be avoided over prolonged periods of operation [10,14,15,44,69,70] . Rangwala studied CO 2 absorption into aqueous sodium hydroxide and aqueous amine solutions using a PP membrane module [10] .  Results showed that PP membranes were wetted by both absorption solutions. Khaisri et al. developed a mathematical model for CO 2 absorption into monoethanolamine (MEA) solutions using PTFE hollow fiber membranes [52] . They suggested the maximum acceptable percentage of membrane wetting was 40% and membrane cartridges should be replaced when wetting exceeds this value in order to maintain the best performance for CO 2 absorption. Rongwong et al.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig:  Mechanism of membrane wetting
texts.text:  Mechanism of membrane wetting

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  mechanism  of  membrane  wetting  is  still  being debated due to a lack of understanding of membranesolvent interaction. Several researchers have proposed different explanations for membrane wetting. Wang et al. investigated the impact of DEA solutions on PP membrane surface morphology and surface tension, and suggested that membrane wetting may be caused by possible chemical reactions between the membranes and DEA that change the hydrophobic characteristics of the system [72] . A similar conclusion was also made by Franco et al. by contacting PP membranes with MEA solutions [73] . However, other researchers believe membrane wetting is a result of physical interaction between the membrane pores and solvents. According to Rangwala, the membrane surface hydrophobicity was possibly modified by trace impurities and ionic species in the solvent, which penetrated into the membrane pores via a necking phenomenon [10] . Lv et al. immersed PP hollow fibers in three different solvents including MEA, methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) and deionized water for up to 90 days [74] . Based on the characterization results of the membrane samples, they proposed an absorption-swelling wetting mechanism in which the diffusion of MEA molecules leads to a swelling of the membrane pores, which decreases the membrane surface hydrophobicity significantly. They suggested that improving membrane surface hydrophobicity maybe an effective method for preventing membrane wetting. Similarly, Mansourizadeh et al. suggested that pore enlargement seems to be the main reason for the wetting of PVDF membranes in chemical absorption [75] .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Solvent screening
texts.text: Solvent screening

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Various inorganic and organic solvents have been studied experimentally for CO 2 absorption in hollow fiber membrane contactors. Inorganic solvents such as deionized water, sodium hydroxide, and potassium carbonate have a high surface tension and do not easily wet common hydrophobic membranes such as PTFE [8,39,76] . However, their CO 2 absorption performance is lower than traditional organic solvents, such as MEA and MDEA [30] . Amine solutions are the most commonly used organic absorption liquids for CO 2 absorption in hollow fiber membrane contactors, since they have high absorption performance and are relatively easy to recover. However, the surface tension of the organic solvents is low when compared with inorganic solvents. As a result, they tend to easily wet hydrophobic membranes including PP, PE, PVDF and even PTFE. In addition, solvent degradation may occur when amine solutions are contacted with other components in the gas streams; for example, impurities in flue gas. The accumulation of degradation products may change the surface tension of amine solutions, which may cause membrane wetting. To date, very little research has been conducted in this area.

texts.orig: Several composite solutions based on the glycinate solution were also studied for CO 2 absorption in PP hollow fiber membrane contactors [25,82,83] . For example, piperazine (PZ) was used as a promoter and added into glycinate salt solution. The overall mass transfer coefficient with the PZ activated glycinate salt solution was found to be 1.3-times that of glycinate salt solution [84,85] . In addition to amino acid salts, 2-amino-2methyl-1-propanol and PZ were also studied as promoters in traditional amine solutions to effectively improve the absorption capacity and absorption rate of CO 2 in hollow fiber membrane contactors [27,50,53,86-89] . However, experimental results have not yet shown that they are able to help prevent membrane wetting.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Membrane surface modification & manufacturing
texts.text: Membrane surface modification & manufacturing

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to reduce membrane wetting, hydrophobic membranes such as PTFE, PP, PE and PVDF have been widely studied for CO 2 absorption in hollow fiber membrane contactors. The performance of the PTFE membranes has been found to be higher than that of PVDF and PP membranes. PTFE membranes resist wetting from absorption liquids and maintain long-term stability of the CO 2 absorption performance [46] . However, PTFE membranes are not commercially available in small diameters and are relatively expensive to make, which limits the economic feasibility of CO 2 absorption in hollow fiber membrane contactors, especially when compared with traditional packed columns.

However, PTFE membranes are not commercially available in small diameters and are relatively expensive to make, which limits the economic feasibility of CO 2 absorption in hollow fiber membrane contactors, especially when compared with traditional packed columns.

texts.orig: Compared with PTFE membranes, PP membranes are affordable and commercially available in a wide range of sizes. Experimental studies indicate that the performance of PP membranes in CO 2 absorption systems is initially comparable to that of PTFE, but over a prolonged period of operation the PP membranes are prone to wetting and the performance drops both rapidly and significantly [10,41,55,72-74] .  Current efforts to reduce or prevent membrane wetting are mainly focused on surface coatings using superior hydrophobic materials [77] .  Franco et al. sputtered PP membranes with an ultrathin PTFE layer in a radio frequency plasma reactor and tested their CO 2 absorption performance using MEA solutions [24] . Experimental results showed the performance of the coated membrane was comparable, or superior, to that of both PTFE membranes and untreated PP membranes over a prolonged period of operation time. Nguyen et  al. designed a dense skin composite PP membrane coated with two glassy polymers (Teflon ®  AF 2400 and poly[1trimethylsilyl]-1-propyne) [91] . A series of CO 2 absorption tests were performed using MEA solutions in a composite PP membrane contactor. Results confirmed that the dense skin had a high CO 2 permeability while providing wetting protection for PP membranes. Chabanon et al. did a comparative analysis of membrane wetting resistance over 1000 h for CO 2 absorption in a 30% MEA solution [92] . PTFE and PP membranes were compared with two novel composite hollow fiber materials: poly(4-methyl-2-pentyne) and a Teflon AF dense skin coated PP membrane. Results showed that in the initial nonwetted condition the composite membranes had a lower CO 2 absorption flux when compared with the PP and PTFE membranes. This decreased permeability was caused by the additional mass transfer resistance due to the dense skin. Interestingly, since the PP and PTFE membranes were partially wetted by MEA, gradually, the absorption flux of the composite membranes became comparable to PTFE membranes and greatly surpassed the PP membranes after 500 h. This type of behavior demonstrated the effective protection of dense skins on membrane wetting.

texts.orig: PVDF membranes are currently considered a promising alternative membrane for CO 2 absorption in hollow fiber membrane contactors because of their relatively high hydrophobicity and reasonable cost. A large amount of research has studied the fabrication of PVDF hollow fiber membranes using the wet/dry phase inversion method. The membrane structure and performance changes with different nonsolvent promoter additives in the spinning dopes. Mansourizadeh and Ismail used lithium chloride (LiCl) as a nonsolvent additive in the polymer dopes for PVDF membrane manufacturing [93] . They reported that the prepared PVDF membrane had a CO 2 flux that was 60% higher than plain PVDF membranes for CO 2 absorption in distilled water. A long-term study (80 h) of CO 2 absorption in sodium hydroxide solutions demonstrated that the CO 2 flux of the prepared PVDF membranes dropped by 23% before stabilizing after 150 h [75] . In addition to LiCl, a number of polymer dope additives have been investigated for manufacturing PVDF membranes with different structures [49,94-96] .  Mansourizadeh and Ismail studied the effect of glycerol, phosphoric acid, ethanol and PE glycol as additives in the spinning dope on PVDF membrane structure and performance [96] . texts.orig: wetting resistance [96] . Meanwhile, PVDF membranes that had ethanol as an additive showed a finger-spongelike structure with high permeability and low wetting resistance. Results from CO 2 absorption in distilled water  showed  that  the  CO 2 flux  in  PVDF/glycerol membranes was higher than that in other manufactured membranes. Atchariyawut et al. manufactured PVDF membranes using three dope solutions containing N -methyl-2-pyrrolidone and different additives, including distilled water, phosphorous acid and glycerol [49] . The manufactured PVDF membranes were used in a gas-liquid membrane contactor for pure CO 2 absorption in distilled water. Experimental results showed that the CO 2 flux of three PVDF membranes could remain almost constant over 15 days of operation. The CO 2 absorption performance was in the order of phosphorous acid > glycerol > distilled water. Additional research results confirmed that the PVDF membranes prepared with phosphoric acid as an additive in spinning dopes could obtain roughly 30% more CO 2 absorption flux in distilled water than that of commercial PTFE membranes and PVDF membranes with LiCl monohydrate as additive [94] .

texts.orig: Lastly, some research groups have found that internal coagulants significantly impact membrane structure and performance via the phase inversion method. Xu et al. fabricated PVDF membranes using different internal coagulants, including water, ethanol-water and dimethylacetamide-water solutions [78] . By studying the membrane cross sections, the composition of internal coagulants was found to have a significant impact on the structure of the inner skin layer. Experimental results showed that PVDF membranes with a skinless inner surface had an equivalent CO 2 absorption performance to that of PTFE membranes. Results from Feng et al. have also demonstrated that the inner skinless surface of PTFE membranes had a lower mass transfer resistance than membranes with a double skin structure [97] .

texts.orig: According to Faiz and Al-Marzouqi, the dimensionless model equations related to the tube, membrane and shell sides could be solved numerically along with appropriate boundary conditions, physical and chemical properties, as well as reaction rate equations, by using a number of mathematical methods [103] .  For example, COMSOL Multiphysics ®  software, which uses the finite element method, was commonly used for numerical solutions of differential equations in the model. A variety of numerical solvers such as DASPK and UMFPACK have been adapted for finite element ana  lysis [81,101,103-106] .  The model developed by Faiz and Al-Marzouqi was solved using COMSOL Multiphysics ®   and  DASPK.  The  model  results  showed excellent agreement with the experimental data for the physical absorption and chemical absorption of CO 2 in a hollow fiber membrane contactor. Additionally, the dimensionless model equations could be solved by using other mathematic methods including the orthogonal collocation method, the Crank-Nicholson method and the Baker and Oliphant method [39,70,107] . It is noted that the developed mathematical model, based on a steady state material balance, can also be applied to membrane contactor system in which the liquid flows on the shell side [106] .

texts.orig: CO2 gas that is produced in the regenerator can be used for secondary industrial processes, such as CCS. The principle of solvent regeneration in membrane contactors is similar to traditional membrane distillation, especially sweep gas membrane distillation. As shown in Figure 9 , the rich solvent and sweep gas stream flow counter-currently on the opposite sides of the membrane. Mass transfer occurs by diffusion across the gas-liquid interface, which is at the mouth of each membrane pore. The mass transfer process in membrane contactors for solvent regeneration is similar to the one for absorption, but the direction of mass transfer is reversed from the rich solution to the sweep gas stream.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The application of hollow fiber membrane contactors for solvent regeneration is an alternative to traditional heating regenerators since it offers high interfacial area per unit volume, independent fluids flow rates, compact size, easy scale-up and a relatively low regeneration temperature [108] . The lower regeneration temperature in membrane contactors, when compared with conventional regenerators, allows for the use of waste heat or low-grade energy, thus dramatically decreasing regeneration energy consumption and capture cost. However, the regeneration rate may be limited by diffusion through the liquid to liquidgas interface at lower regeneration temperatures [109] . The integration of membrane-based technology and vacuum regeneration process may be an effective and novel method for improving the mass transfer driving force of desorption. According to experimental results from Yan et al. [109] and Fang et al. [110] ,  membrane vacuum regeneration technology is superior to conventional heating regeneration technology in terms of regeneration efficiency and loss of absorption solvent.

texts.orig: Since membrane distillation is frequently operated at elevated temperatures, thermal stability is an important factor for membrane selection. Under high temperatures, membrane fibers may not be able to resist degradation or decomposition. To date there has been very little research on the effect of high temperature on the thermal stability of hollow fiber membranes for the regeneration of CO 2 -loaded solvents. texts.orig: Experimental  results  have  found  that  operating conditions play a significant role in the CO 2 stripping efficiency in hollow fiber membrane contactors. CO 2 stripping efficiency increases with an increase in the initial liquid concentration, CO 2 loading in the solution, liquid-phase temperature and liquid flow rate [108,112-114] .  The  desorption  process  was  controlled by diffusion and chemical reaction in the liquid film of the liquid phase and the liquid-filled pores of the membrane [112] . In addition, according to Naim et al. improving the structure of the PVDF hollow fiber membranes can increase CO 2 stripping efficiency [5] .

texts.orig: Similar  to  CO 2 absorption,  researchers  have observed the phenomena of membrane wetting when applying hollow fiber membrane contactors for solvent regeneration [113,115,116] . According to Simoni et al. , the stripping of CO 2 from a potassium carbonate solution was studied by using two types of polymeric flat sheet microporous membranes: a PTFE membrane and an asymmetric polyether sulfone membrane with a proprietary hydrophobic surface coating [17] .  The mass transfer performance of the asymmetric membrane increased as the temperature increased from 60 to 100°C. However, the performance of PTFE decreased at the elevated temperatures due to membrane wetting.

texts.orig: Significant pilot-scale applications
texts.text: Significant pilot-scale applications

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  recent  years,  gas  absorption  using  microporous hollow fiber membrane contactors has been attracting  more  attention  for  CO 2 capture  from  various industrial gas streams such as flue gas and natural gas. Several research efforts have been done at both the bench- and pilot-scale levels. Some of the larger pilot-scale studies that used membrane contactors for CO2 capture were successful in operation and reported positive results.
texts.text: In  recent  years,  gas  absorption  using  microporous hollow fiber membrane contactors has been attracting  more  attention  for  CO 2 capture  from  various industrial gas streams such as flue gas and natural gas. Several research efforts have been done at both the bench- and pilot-scale levels. Some of the larger pilot-scale studies that used membrane contactors for CO2 capture were successful in operation and reported positive results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Kværner Process Systems (KPS; Sandefjord, Norway) developed new membrane gas-liquid contactors and used them for CO 2 capture from exhaust gas and natural  gas [118] .  They  built  a  large  pilot-scale  gas absorption membrane contactor at the Statoil Gas Terminal at Kårstø K-laboratory for exhaust gas treatment from a gas engine. The pilot unit was operated from 1998 to 2001 for a total of 7000 h and 85% (195 kg/h) of the CO 2 was removed from the exhaust gas flow. Then, compared with traditional amine treating processes, the membrane contactor technology for CO 2 capture on the total offshore platform could potentially reduce 35-40% of the capital cost and save 38-42% of the operating costs. KPS also tested a pilot membrane contactor unit at a large gas terminal in Scotland (UK) to prove the laboratory results and test membrane life. The membrane contactor was fed with an activated MDEA solution. The test lasted from April 1998 to October 1999. A total of eight membrane modules were studied. The results showed that the membrane modules could operate approximately 5000 h without a decrease in membrane performance. Another pilot-scale test by KPS was carried out at the Shell/Tejas Gas Fandango gas plant in Texas (USA). Natural gas with 6% CO 2 was treated in a membrane gas-liquid contactor by using a physical solvent (Morphysorb ® ). Long-term duration test results indicated no deterioration in membrane performance.

texts.text: Future perspective

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Compared with conventional CO 2 absorption technologies, hollow fiber membrane contactors have a few advantages, including their high interfacial area per unit volume and independent liquid and gas flow rates. Additionally, they do not have any of the operating problems suffered in conventional technologies, such as flooding, foaming and entrainment. However, most of the work with membrane contactors for CO 2 capture has been done at the laboratory-scale level. While there have been a few pilot-scale applications that achieved positive results, this field is still in the experimental stage and needs further advancement in order to be applied as a full-scale industrial technology. In order to achieve this goal, the following challenges and recommendations need to be addressed in future work:
texts.text: Compared with conventional CO 2 absorption technologies, hollow fiber membrane contactors have a few advantages, including their high interfacial area per unit volume and independent liquid and gas flow rates. texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Many researchers have studied membrane wetting and found it was mainly influenced by the balance between the transmembrane pressure and breakthrough pressure. As indicated in Equation 29 ,  the breakthrough pressure depends on the membrane properties, the surface tension of absorption liquid and the contact angle between the absorption liquid and the membrane surface. However, there is still a dispute among several researchers on the mechanism of membrane wetting when using different solvents and membrane materials. Future research should focus on studying the membrane wetting process and the interaction between the solvents and membranes during long periods of operation. In order to maintain the long-term stability and performance in hollow fiber membrane contactors, the mechanism of membrane wetting needs to be well understood;

texts.orig:  Most previous research projects have been conducted at the laboratory scale under ambient temperature and low pressure. Pure CO 2 , or its mixture with N 2 , was commonly used as the gas stream. In order to commercialize this technology, further work must be done with real gas streams, such as a flue gas or natural gas, in order to test the effects of impurities (H 2 S, NOx and so on) and high temperature on membrane performance. Additionally, the particulates produced during the combustion of fossil fuels can foul the membrane or plug the module, dramatically reducing the  membrane  performance.  Future  work  on gas-liquid membrane absorption process design must account for this issue;

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Conclusion
texts.text: Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The major research findings from projects on CO 2 absorption and solvent regeneration using hollow fiber membrane contactors have been reviewed in this article. Hollow fiber membrane contactors for CO 2 capture have been considered a promising alternative to traditional absorption technologies, such as packed columns. Membrane contactors combine the advantages of both absorption technologies and membrane separation technologies. Previous research results have demonstrated this technology is not only feasible, but that it has the potential to perform better than packed columns.
